answer:
Definition

Security Scans in CI/CD automatically detect vulnerabilities in your code and dependencies before they reach production. This includes dependency vulnerability scanning (known CVEs in npm packages), static analysis (code patterns that lead to security issues), secret detection (API keys committed to git), and container image scanning.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, a GitHub Actions workflow runs npm audit and trivy on every PR. It caught a critical vulnerability in an old version of jsonwebtoken (CVE-2022-23529) that could allow JWT forgery. The automated scan flagged it before the PR was merged, and the fix was a simple npm update jsonwebtoken.

Visual Guide

Code Example

# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Scans
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  dependency-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: 'npm' }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm audit --audit-level=high
        # Fails if any high/critical vulnerabilities found

  secret-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }  # full history for secret scanning
      - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        # Scans git history for API keys, passwords, tokens

  container-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: docker build -t hr-leave-system .
      - uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: hr-leave-system
          severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
          exit-code: '1'  # fail if critical/high CVEs found

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Running npm audit with --audit-level=low — generates hundreds of low-severity alerts that developers ignore, causing alert fatigue.

Mitigation: Set --audit-level=high to only flag critical and high severity issues. Review moderate/low issues monthly in a separate process, not on every PR.

Definition

Security Scans solve the "unknown vulnerability" problem. Your code might be secure, but your dependencies aren't. The average Node.js project has 200+ transitive dependencies — any one could contain a known vulnerability. Automated scans catch these before they're exploited.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, a Dependabot alert flagged a prototype pollution vulnerability in a nested dependency of express. Without automated scanning, this would have gone unnoticed until exploited. The fix was updating Express from 4.17.1 to 4.18.2 — a minor version bump that took 5 minutes.

Problems Solved

  • Known CVEs: Automatically detect and alert on published vulnerabilities in dependencies
  • Secret leaks: Prevent API keys, database passwords, and tokens from being committed to git
  • Container vulnerabilities: Scan Docker images for OS-level CVEs in the base image
  • Code quality: Static analysis catches SQL injection, XSS, and other OWASP Top 10 issues

Code Example

# Comprehensive security pipeline
name: Security
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 8 * * 1'  # Weekly Monday scan

jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # 1. Dependency vulnerabilities
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm audit --audit-level=high --omit=dev

      # 2. Static code analysis for security
      - run: npx eslint src/ --rule 'no-eval:error'
      # Check for common vulnerabilities:
      # - eval() usage
      # - innerHTML without sanitization
      # - SQL string concatenation

      # 3. License compliance
      - run: npx license-checker --failOn 'GPL-3.0'

      # 4. Secret scanning with trufflehog
      - uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          extra_args: --only-verified

  # Dependabot config (.github/dependabot.yml)
  # version: 2
  # updates:
  #   - package-ecosystem: "npm"
  #     directory: "/"
  #     schedule:
  #       interval: "weekly"
  #     open-pull-requests-limit: 5

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Ignoring npm audit findings because they're in dev dependencies. Dev dependencies can still be exploited during build time (supply chain attacks).

Mitigation: Audit ALL dependencies, not just production ones. Use npm audit --omit=dev as the strict gate (blocks PRs), and npm audit (all deps) as a weekly report.

Top 3 Artifact Versioning Mistakes

Mistake 1: Using "latest" Tag for Docker Images

Deploying myapp:latest means you never know which version is running. Rollbacks are impossible.

# ❌ BAD: Always push as "latest"
docker build -t myapp:latest .
docker push myapp:latest
# Which version is running in production? Nobody knows.

# ✅ GOOD: Semantic versioning + git SHA
VERSION=$(git describe --tags --always)
docker build -t myapp:$VERSION -t myapp:latest .
docker push myapp:$VERSION
docker push myapp:latest
# Production runs myapp:v1.2.3 — rollback to myapp:v1.2.2

Mistake 2: Not Linking Artifacts to Git Commits

Can't trace a deployed artifact back to the exact source code that produced it.

# ❌ BAD: No traceability
docker build -t myapp:v1 .
# v1 could be any commit. Which code is in production?

# ✅ GOOD: Include git SHA in image labels
docker build \
  --label "git.sha=$GITHUB_SHA" \
  --label "git.branch=$GITHUB_REF" \
  --label "build.date=$(date -u +'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')" \
  -t myapp:$(git rev-parse --short HEAD) .
# Now: docker inspect myapp:abc123 → shows exact commit

Mistake 3: Not Cleaning Up Old Artifacts

Container registries fill up with hundreds of old images, costing money and making it hard to find the right version.

# ❌ BAD: Never clean up
# Registry has 500 images, 100GB storage, $50/month

# ✅ GOOD: Retention policy — keep last 10 versions
# In GitHub Actions:
- uses: actions/delete-package-versions@v4
  with:
    package-name: 'hr-leave-system'
    min-versions-to-keep: 10
    package-type: 'container'

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, every Docker image is tagged with the git SHA and semantic version: hr-leave:v1.3.2-abc123f. When a production bug is found, the team can check exactly which commit is deployed, rollback to the previous version (v1.3.1), and fix forward with confidence.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Manual version bumping — developers forget to update the version, leading to duplicate versions or skipped numbers.

Mitigation: Automate versioning with conventional commits + semantic-release. Commit messages like fix: bump patch, feat: bump minor, BREAKING CHANGE: bump major.

Definition

Artifact Versioning is the practice of assigning unique, traceable version identifiers to build outputs — Docker images, npm packages, compiled binaries — so you can always know exactly what's deployed, roll back to a previous version, and trace any artifact to its source code.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, every merge to main triggers a CI pipeline that builds a Docker image tagged v{major}.{minor}.{patch}-{gitSHA}. The deployment manifest references the exact tag. When a bug was found in v1.4.0, the team instantly rolled back to v1.3.2 by updating the tag in the deployment config — zero downtime, zero guesswork.

Visual Guide

Code Example

# GitHub Actions: Automated versioning + Docker push
name: Build & Publish
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }

      # Auto-version based on conventional commits
      - id: version
        run: |
          # Get latest tag
          LATEST=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 2>/dev/null || echo "v0.0.0")
          # Check commit messages since last tag
          if git log $LATEST..HEAD --oneline | grep -q "^.*feat:"; then
            NEW=$(echo $LATEST | awk -F. '{print $1"."$2+1".0"}')
          else
            NEW=$(echo $LATEST | awk -F. '{print $1"."$2"."$3+1}')
          fi
          SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
          echo "version=$NEW" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "full_tag=$NEW-$SHA" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - run: |
          docker build \
            --label "version=${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}" \
            --label "git.sha=${{ github.sha }}" \
            -t ghcr.io/manoj0724/hr-leave:${{ steps.version.outputs.full_tag }} \
            -t ghcr.io/manoj0724/hr-leave:latest .

      - run: docker push --all-tags ghcr.io/manoj0724/hr-leave

      - run: git tag ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
      - run: git push --tags

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Rebuilding artifacts for production instead of promoting the tested artifact. If you rebuild, the production binary might differ from what was tested.

Mitigation: Build once, deploy everywhere. The same Docker image tested in staging is promoted to production — no rebuild. Only the environment variables change.

Definition

Canary Releases solve the deployment risk problem. Instead of deploying a new version to all users at once, you route a small percentage of traffic (1-5%) to the new version while the rest stays on the current version. If the canary shows errors, you roll back before most users are affected.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, a new version with updated leave calculation logic was deployed as a canary to 5% of users. Monitoring showed a 2% error rate on the canary (vs. 0% on stable) — the new logic had a bug with leap year calculations. The canary was rolled back, affecting only ~10 users instead of 200+.

Problems Solved

  • Blast radius reduction: Bugs affect 5% of users instead of 100%
  • Real-world validation: Test with real traffic and real data that staging can't replicate
  • Confidence: Gradual rollout (5% → 25% → 50% → 100%) with monitoring at each step
  • Fast rollback: Shift traffic back to stable in seconds, no redeployment needed

Visual Guide

Code Example

# Simple canary with nginx weight-based routing
# nginx.conf
upstream backend {
  server stable-v1.3:3000 weight=95;  # 95% traffic
  server canary-v1.4:3000 weight=5;   # 5% traffic
}

# GitHub Actions: Deploy canary
name: Canary Deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      canary_weight:
        description: 'Traffic percentage for canary'
        default: '5'

jobs:
  deploy-canary:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: |
          # Deploy new version as canary
          kubectl set image deployment/canary \
            app=hr-leave:${{ github.sha }}

          # Set traffic split
          kubectl annotate ingress hr-leave \
            nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary="true" \
            nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight="${{ inputs.canary_weight }}"

      - run: |
          # Monitor for 10 minutes
          sleep 600
          ERROR_RATE=$(curl -s prometheus/api/v1/query \
            --data-urlencode 'query=rate(http_errors_total{version="canary"}[5m])')
          if [ "$ERROR_RATE" -gt "0.01" ]; then
            echo "Canary error rate too high: $ERROR_RATE"
            kubectl annotate ingress hr-leave \
              nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight="0"
            exit 1
          fi

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Canary without monitoring. Deploying 5% traffic to the new version but not watching error rates, latency, or user complaints — you miss the signal.

Mitigation: Set up automated canary analysis: compare error rates and latency between canary and stable. Auto-rollback if the canary's error rate is 2× higher than stable. Tools: Prometheus + Grafana alerts.

Top 3 Canary Release Mistakes

Mistake 1: Canary Without Proper Monitoring

Deploying a canary but not comparing its metrics to the stable version. You can't detect problems you're not measuring.

# ❌ BAD: Deploy canary and hope for the best
kubectl set image deployment/canary app=hr-leave:v1.4
echo "Canary deployed! Check Slack if users complain."

# ✅ GOOD: Automated canary analysis
# Compare canary vs stable metrics:
# - Error rate: canary 0.5% vs stable 0.1% → 5× worse → ROLLBACK
# - p99 latency: canary 800ms vs stable 200ms → 4× worse → ROLLBACK
# - Success rate: canary 99.5% vs stable 99.9% → INVESTIGATE

Mistake 2: Canary Percentage Too High

Starting with 25-50% traffic defeats the purpose. If the canary has a bug, a quarter of your users are affected.

# ❌ BAD: 50% canary from the start
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "50"
# Bug affects 50% of users — not much better than a full deploy!

# ✅ GOOD: Gradual rollout
# Step 1: 1% for 10 minutes (smoke test)
# Step 2: 5% for 30 minutes (basic validation)
# Step 3: 25% for 1 hour (broader validation)
# Step 4: 100% (full promotion)

Mistake 3: Database Schema Changes Breaking the Stable Version

The canary runs new code against a modified database schema — but the stable version also reads from the same database and breaks.

# ❌ BAD: Canary migration drops a column
# Canary v1.4: ALTER TABLE leaves DROP COLUMN old_status;
# Stable v1.3: SELECT old_status FROM leaves; → ERROR!

# ✅ GOOD: Backward-compatible migrations
# Step 1: Canary v1.4 stops READING old_status (still exists)
# Step 2: Promote canary to 100%
# Step 3: v1.5 drops old_status column (no version reads it)

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a canary release of the new notification system sent duplicate Socket.io messages. The 5% canary meant only ~10 employees received duplicates. Monitoring caught the doubled notification_sent metric within 5 minutes, and the canary was rolled back before most users noticed.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Session stickiness causing some users to ALWAYS hit the canary. If those users have an unusual usage pattern, the canary metrics are skewed.

Mitigation: Use random traffic splitting (not session-based) for canary routing. This distributes the risk evenly across all user types and usage patterns.

Top 3 Rollback Automation Mistakes

Mistake 1: No Rollback Plan at All

The only "rollback" is to push a revert commit and wait for CI/CD to rebuild and redeploy — taking 10-30 minutes while production is broken.

# ❌ BAD: Rollback = revert commit + full CI/CD pipeline
git revert HEAD
git push  # triggers 15-minute CI pipeline
# Production is broken for 15+ minutes!

# ✅ GOOD: Instant rollback by redeploying previous artifact
# Keep previous Docker image always available
kubectl set image deployment/hr-leave app=hr-leave:v1.3.2
# Rollback in 30 seconds — no rebuild needed

Mistake 2: Not Testing Rollback Procedures

The rollback script exists but nobody has tested it. In a real incident, it fails because the database migration can't be reversed.

# ❌ BAD: Untested rollback
# rollback.sh (written 6 months ago, never tested)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/hr-leave
# Does this work? Is the previous image still in the registry?
# Does the database support downgrade?

# ✅ GOOD: Monthly rollback drills
# 1. Deploy to staging
# 2. Run rollback script
# 3. Verify app works on previous version
# 4. Verify database is compatible
# Document results in runbook

Mistake 3: Database Migrations That Can't Be Reversed

A forward migration drops a column. Rollback deploys the old code that reads from the dropped column — crash.

# ❌ BAD: Destructive migration with no rollback path
ALTER TABLE leaves DROP COLUMN legacy_status;
# Old code: SELECT legacy_status FROM leaves; → ERROR

# ✅ GOOD: Two-phase migration
# Phase 1 (deploy v1.4): Stop reading legacy_status, keep column
# Phase 2 (after v1.4 is stable): Drop column in v1.5
# Rollback from v1.4 → v1.3 is safe because column still exists

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, a deployment broke the leave approval endpoint. The team rolled back by redeploying the previous Docker image tag (v1.3.2) — taking 30 seconds. The database migration was backward-compatible (added a nullable column), so the old code worked fine.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Automated rollback based on a single metric spike. A momentary CPU spike triggers rollback unnecessarily.

Mitigation: Use compound health checks: rollback only when error rate > 1% AND p99 latency > 500ms AND for at least 3 minutes. Single metric spikes can be transient.

Definition

Rollback Automation is the ability to instantly revert a production deployment to the previous working version when issues are detected — without manual intervention, without rebuilding, and without data loss. It's your safety net for every deployment.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, every deployment keeps the previous 3 Docker image versions in the registry. The rollback script is a single command: kubectl rollout undo deployment/hr-leave. When a deployment broke the Socket.io notification system, the on-call developer rolled back in 30 seconds using the documented runbook.

Visual Guide

Code Example

# Automated rollback in GitHub Actions
name: Deploy with Auto-Rollback
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # Build and push new image
      - run: |
          docker build -t hr-leave:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push hr-leave:${{ github.sha }}

      # Deploy new version
      - run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/hr-leave \
            app=hr-leave:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl rollout status deployment/hr-leave --timeout=120s

      # Health check — rollback if unhealthy
      - run: |
          for i in $(seq 1 10); do
            STATUS=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.hr-leave.com/health)
            if [ "$STATUS" != "200" ]; then
              echo "Health check failed! Status: $STATUS"
              kubectl rollout undo deployment/hr-leave
              echo "Rolled back to previous version"
              exit 1
            fi
            sleep 10
          done
          echo "Deployment healthy!"

      # Notify team
      - if: failure()
        run: |
          curl -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK -d '{
            "text": "⚠️ Deploy rolled back! Check logs."
          }'

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Rollback that doesn't account for database migrations. The old code version needs to work with the new database schema.

Mitigation: Always make database migrations backward-compatible. Add columns as nullable, never rename or drop columns in the same release as the code change. Use the expand-contract pattern.
